meta.professor_methodology: Emphasis on superposition principle and vector nature of electric fields
meta.description: Coulomb's law, electric field calculations, and superposition for point charges and continuous distributions

patterns.name: Electric Field from Point Charges
patterns.recognition_signal: E-field at a point due to one or more point charges
patterns.logic: Each point charge creates radial E-field; total field is vector sum

efficiency_trick.name: Symmetry Cancellation
steps: Identify components that cancel by symmetry

pitfalls.name: Source vs test charge confusion
pitfalls.description: E-field formula uses SOURCE charge Q, not test charge q

pitfalls.name: Direction errors
pitfalls.description: E points away from positive, toward negative charges

worked_example.title: Two Charge E-Field
worked_example.problem_latex: Find E at midpoint between +q and -q separated by distance d

decision_tree.question: Is this about force or field?
decision_tree.yes: F = qE for force, E = kQ/r² for field

professor_tips: Always draw the E-field vector at the point of interest before calculating
Superposition means calculate each contribution separately, then add vectors
Remember: E-field exists whether or not there's a test charge to feel it
For continuous distributions, set up the integral and look for symmetry

confidence_checklist: I can apply Coulomb's law to find force between point charges
I understand that E-field is force per unit charge
I can calculate E-field from multiple point charges using superposition
I know the direction conventions for E-field
I can set up integrals for continuous charge distributions
